sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 170, level 2.
Valid actions right now: LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'LEFT': 'Moves player left ~6 columns', 'RIGHT': 'Moves player right ~6 columns', 'MOUSE': 'Clicks on position. Clicking green blocks collects them but game over after 8-9 total green clicks. Clicking dots or purple blocks adds 1 purple pixel to bottom bar. Player movement (LEFT/RIGHT) does NOT reset game over counter.', 'ACTION7': "Invalid action - gives 'Unknown action' error despite being in valid_actions list"}
- Object taxonomy: {'background': 'black with gray dots', 'green_blocks': 'light green 21-pixel blocks in 2x7 grid at rows 25-30 and 31-36, cols 13-52', 'purple_blocks_top': '3 purple blocks at rows 7-10, cols 14, 20, 26', 'dots': 'yellow/white dots at row 11 below purple blocks - clicking adds purple pixels to bottom bar', 'player': 'blue+yellow small object', 'platforms': 'large sky blue regions'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_bar': 'purple+white at row 63, resets on game over. Filling to 9 does NOT unlock green collection.'}
- Win pattern: {'level1': 'Collect green blocks with MOUSE, move LEFT toward pink cross to complete level', 'level2': 'UNKNOWN - all attempts to collect 14 green blocks fail with game over after 8-9 clicks'}
- Level log: {'level1': 'Completed in 50 actions - collect greens with MOUSE, approach pink cross, LEFT to complete', 'level2': 'Multiple game overs. All strategies failed: batch clicks, bar filling, interleaving with dots, player movement between clicks. Need fundamentally different approach.'}
- Cross-level notes: Level 2: Game over triggers after 8-9 green block clicks regardless of bar state, dot interleaving, or player movement. ACTION7 is invalid. The solution must involve a mechanic not yet discovered - possibly clicking non-green objects, using the player character differently, or a specific sequence/order.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 119 actions; a strong score needs about 48 or fewer. You are 2.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK so the bar has 14 purple pixels at positions 0-13, and 50 white pixels at positions 14-63. The bar is NOT accumulating continuously - it resets on game over but the purple pixels persist within a session.
Wait, I see 4 bar nodes:
- 3 purple blocks at top (12 pixels each) - these are the purple blocks, not bar
- 1 bar node with 14 pixels at row 63, cols 0-13
So the bar has 14 purple out of 64. The bar seems to track the number of green blocks (14)!
This is a key insight. The bar has 14 purple pixels, matching the 14 green blocks. Maybe the bar represents progress toward collecting all 14 greens.
But I can only click 8-9 greens before game over. What if there's a way to collect greens without clicking them directly? Or what if the game over is actually the intended path and I need to collect 8-9 greens, then do something else?
Wait - let me reconsider. The bar has 14 purple pixels. Maybe I need to fill the bar completely (64 purple) by clicking non-green things, and THEN the greens become collectible without game over?
Or maybe the 14 purple pixels correspond to the 14 green blocks, and I need to click each green block to turn its corresponding bar pixel purple? But the bar already shows 14 purple...
Actually, I think the bar started with 14 purple pixels (matching 14 greens) and I've been adding more with my clicks. Let me check - the bar node shows 14 pixels at cols 0-13. But I've made many clicks that should have added more.
Hmm, wait. Let me re-examine. The bar node has 14 pixels total, at positions 0-13. But I've clicked many times. Maybe the bar resets on game over?
Let me check the history to see if the bar resets on game over. If it does, then the 14 purple pixels are the starting state, representing the 14 green blocks to collect.
New hypothesis: The bar starts with 14 purple (one per green block). When you collect a green, its corresponding bar pixel changes color or disappears. The goal is to collect all 14 greens, turning all bar pixels from purple to something else.
But I can only collect 8-9 before game over. Unless... the game over is part of the mechanic and I need to survive it somehow?
Or maybe I need to click the green blocks in a specific order that corresponds to the bar positions?
Let me try a completely new approach: what if I click on the bar itself? Maybe clicking bar pixels does something special.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Bar has 14 purple pixels at cols 0-13, matching 14 green blocks!
# Key insight: bar represents green block collection progress
# Hypothesis: clicking bar pixels might do something special

# Let me check if bar resets on game over by looking at history
# Also try clicking on the bar itself

# First, let me check the bar in the earliest frames vs now
if len(history) > 50:
    early_seg = history[0].frame.segmentation
    early_bar = [n for n in early_seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'p' and n['boundary'][0][0] == 63]
    print(f"Early bar nodes: {len(early_bar)}, pixels: {[n['pixels'] for n in early_bar]}")

# Try clicking on the bar (purple section)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 63, 'col': 7}])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"ACTION7": "Invalid", "LEFT": "Moves player left ~6 columns", "MOUSE": "Any click adds 1 purple to bar. Green: game over persists.", "RIGHT": "Moves player right ~6 columns"}, "cross_level_notes": "Bar has 14 purple = 14 green blocks. Testing bar clicks.", "hud_map": {"bottom_bar": "14 purple at cols 0-13, 50 white at 14-63"}, "level_log": {"level2": "Bar matches green count. Testing bar clicks."}, "win_pattern": {"level1": "Collect green blocks, move LEFT toward pink cross"}}, "level_tier": {"current_plan": "Click bar to test if it triggers something", "failed_probes": ["Green collection, LEFT, platform clicks, dot clicks"], "goal_model": "Bar represents green collection progress", "recent_findings": ["Bar has exactly 14 purple pixels matching 14 green blocks"], "world_model": "14 greens, bar 14/64 purple (14=green count)"}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
